Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd sometimes sit down with my notes or readings when I felt like it, but there wasn't any real structure to it. Most weeks I'd study maybe three hours a day on average, but it was scattered and unfocused. I'd often find myself just going through the motions without really absorbing anything.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. I'd cram a bit more when deadlines were close, but even then I wasn't super consistent. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, other weeks I'd put in more hours just because I had nothing better to do. It never felt like a proper schedule, more like reacting to whatever was happening.
Q3: Usually I'd log in because I got a notification about something due or because I vaguely remembered I should probably check what was going on. Sometimes I'd log in out of boredom, scroll around for a bit, then close it without doing much. It wasn't like I was excited to be there or anything.
Q4: Not really, no. There were a few weeks early on where I tried to make it a habit, checking in every couple days, but that faded quickly. It never became automatic for me. I'd forget about it for days and then suddenly remember I had courses to deal with.
Q5: The reading materials were probably the most useful since I prefer reading and writing things down anyway. If there was a good PDF or article, I could work with that. Videos were okay but I'd zone out halfway through most of the time. The discussion posts occasionally had useful stuff from other students.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the optional stuff and honestly even some of the required assignments. The interactive activities felt tedious and I'd skip them. Videos longer than fifteen minutes I'd usually avoid or just let play in the background. It just felt like too much effort for not enough payoff.
Q7: If something was quick and clearly connected to what I needed for exams, I'd do it. Anything that felt like busywork or took forever to complete just made me lose interest immediately. I needed to see the point right away or I'd mentally check out.
Q8: Optional stuff might as well not exist for me. If it's not required, I'm probably not doing it unless I'm incredibly bored. Required activities I'd at least attempt, even if I didn't finish them properly. There's just no motivation to do extra when the mandatory stuff already feels like a drag.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download PDFs and readings and then study from those on my laptop without being connected to anything. I'd also make notes by hand sometimes, which obviously doesn't show up anywhere. A lot of my actual studying happened away from the LMS.
Q10: There were times I'd spend an hour or two reading through downloaded course materials or reviewing my handwritten notes, and none of that would register online. I'd also think about concepts while doing other things, like walking or whatever. The LMS only sees clicks, not actual thinking.
Q11: All the time. I'd download something once, maybe watch a video once, and then work from my saved copies. I didn't see the point in going back to the LMS when I already had what I needed on my device. It was just easier that way.
Q12: Around midterms I just felt completely bored and disconnected from everything. The material wasn't grabbing me, and I had other stuff going on with my extracurriculars. It became harder to care when nothing felt particularly interesting or urgent.
Q13: Fatigue was a big one. Some days I'd be too tired to focus on anything meaningful, so I'd just skim through stuff without really engaging. When I had other responsibilities, the course would just slide down my priority list. Confusion about what was actually important also made me disengage.
Q14: Oh, plenty of times. I'd open readings or videos and just stare at them without really processing anything. My mind would wander, I'd be thinking about something else entirely. Technically I was on the LMS, but mentally I was somewhere else completely.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they mattered. It's like I'd tell myself I'd do it later and then later never came until it was too late. I think I just couldn't make myself care enough in the moment, and the consequences felt too far away to worry about.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. Without a deadline breathing down my neck, I wouldn't do anything. Even then, I'd usually start way too late and end up submitting rushed work or not submitting at all. It's a bad cycle but that's how it went.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change much for me. By the time I got it, I'd already moved on mentally. If it was positive, cool, but it didn't make me want to do more. If it was critical, I'd feel a bit bad but not enough to actually change my approach.
Q18: The structure felt pretty loose, which sounds good but actually made it easier for me to drift. If things were more rigid with clearer checkpoints, maybe I would've stayed on track better. The flexibility just gave me room to procrastinate endlessly.
Q19: The gamified task thing they introduced was actually kind of interesting. It made things feel less boring for a bit and gave me something different to engage with. But overall, the course design didn't do much to pull me in. More variety or interactive elements might have helped.
Q20: I think that's probably people like me who log in, click around, maybe start things but never finish. You can spend time on the platform without actually producing anything meaningful. It's easy to look busy without being productive when you're just not that into it.
Q21: That kind of describes me somewhat. I didn't engage much with the LMS but I still passed because I could read and retain enough from the materials I did look at. Some people just learn differently or more efficiently outside the system. The LMS isn't the only way to learn.
Q22: Logs can show when you clicked and how long you were on a page, but they can't show if you were actually paying attention or learning anything. They miss all the offline studying, the thinking, the conversations with classmates. It's a very limited picture of what engagement actually looks like.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it. It's not just showing up or clicking through things. I wasn't really engaged this semester if I'm honest. I was just doing the minimum to get by without any real investment.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I was bored out of my mind most of the time, or that I was dealing with low motivation throughout the course. It wouldn't capture the offline reading I did or the fact that I actually understood more than my assignment completion suggests. Numbers don't tell the whole story.
